coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive and selective detection of MnO4 − in aqueous solution with fluorescent microgels
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Zumei
Xue, Jinqiao
Wen, Bin
Ji, Weiming
Du, Binyang
Nie, Jingjing
coredata.dc:description: The novel functional microgels, namely PNI-MSPC microgels, were fabricated via surfactant free emulsion polymerization (SFEP) by using N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAm) as the monomer and a home-designed dichlorinated 1,1′-di[4-(vinyl)benzyl]-4,4′-pyridinium salt (MSPC2+.2Cl−) as the cross-linker. The PNI-MSPC microgels exhibited not only thermo-sensitivity but also redox actuatable hydrodynamic diameter and fluorescence properties. The PNI-MSPC microgels were successfully applied as the fluorescent sensor for ultrasensitive and selective detection of trace MnO4- anions in aqueous solutions. The presence of MnO4- anions led to the significant fluorescence quenching of the PNI-MSPC microgels with a fast response time of 5 min. The limit of detection (D L) for MnO4- anions can reach 25 nM with a linear concentration range of 0–50 μM. The co-existence of 16 other species of anions or 17 species of metal ions did not interfere with the detection of trace MnO4- anions in aqueous solutions. The potential applications of the PNI-MSPC microgels for sensing trace MnO4- anions in real world samples were also validated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Detection of MnO4 −
Functional microgels
Fluorescence response
Redox actuation

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Redox actuation of size and fluorescence of PNI-MSPC microgels
Label-free detection of trace MnO4 − anions with PNI-MSPC microgels

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Representative TEM image of the PNI-MSPC microgels. (b) The hydrodynamic diameter (D h) of PNI-MSPC microgels measured by DLS as a function of temperature. (c) Zeta potential of PNI-MSPC microgels as a function of temperature. The concentration of PNI-MSPC microgels C MG was 0.426 mg/m L.

(a) FTIR spectra of MSPC2+.2Cl− powders and freeze-dried PNI-MSPC microgels. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of MSPC2+.2Cl− aqueous solutions and PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions. (c) Fluorescence spectra of 0.05 mM MSPC2+.2Cl− aqueous solutions and the PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions with the concentration of viologen moieties of 0.05 mM. The concentration of PNI-MSPC microgels C MG was 0.426 mg/m L. The insets show the photos of MSPC2+.2Cl- aqueous solutions and PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions under 365 nm UV light irradiation.

(a) D h variation of the PNI-MSPC microgels after addition of 10 μ L Na2S2O4 fresh aqueous solutions with various initial concentrations from 0.1 to 0.5 M, which gave the final concentrations of Na2S2O4 in the microgel suspensions from 1 to 2.5 mM. (b) Lifetimes of the shrunk microgels as a function of final Na2S2O4 concentrations in the microgel suspensions. (c) Self-regulated size changes of PNI-MSPC microgels by repeated addition of Na2S2O4 (10 μ L, initial concentration of 0.2 M). (d) D h variation of PNI-MSPC microgels upon addition of Na2S2O4 (10 μ L, initial concentration of 0.2 M) for six cycles. The amount of PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions was 2 m L with concentration C MG of 4.26 mg/m L and the corresponding concentration of viologen moieties was 0.5 mM.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of PNI-MSPC microgel aqueous suspensions before and after addition of Na2S2O4 (10 μ L with concentration of 0.2 M). (b) Fluorescence intensity changes of PNI-MSPC microgels as a function of time after addition of 10 μ L Na2S2O4 aqueous solutions with various initial concentrations. The legend gave the final concentrations of Na2S2O4 in the microgel suspensions. (c) Reversible fluorescence changes of PNI-MSPC microgels upon repeated addition of Na2S2O4 (10 μ L with concentration of 0.2 M) for five cycles. (d) Fluorescence intensity changes of PNI-MSPC microgels within five cycles. (e) Confocal fluorescence images of PNI-MSPC microgels at given times after addition of Na2S2O4 (10 μ L with concentration of 0.2 M). Scale bars were 20 μm. The amount of PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions was 2 m L with concentration C MG = 0.426 mg/m L and the corresponding concentration of viologen moieties was 0.05 mM.

Relative fluorescence intensity changes of PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions before and after separately adding various metal cations (a) and anions (b). (c) Fluorescence response of PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions upon the addition of different concentrations of MnO4 − anions. (d) Modified Stern-Volmer plot of I0/I of PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions versus [MnO4 −], where I0 and I were the fluorescence intensities of PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions in the absence and presence of MnO4 −, respectively. The solid line was the linear-fit curve with equation of I0/I = K SV[Q] + C. The amount of PNI-MSPC microgel suspension was 2 m L with concentration C MG of 0.426 mg/m L and the corresponding concentration of viologen moieties was 0.05 mM.

(a) Time-based fluorescence intensity changes of PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions at 25, 35 and 50 °C, respectively, before and after addition of 50 μM KMnO4 as a function of time. (b) Fluorescent spectra of PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions at 25, 35 and 50 °C, respectively, before (solid curves) and after (dot curves) addition of 50 μM KMnO4. (c) Relative fluorescence intensity at maximum emission wavelength of PNI-MSPC microgels before and after addition of 50 μM KMnO4 as a function of temperature. The amount of PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions was 2 m L with concentration C MG = 0.426 mg/m L and the corresponding concentration of viologen moieties was 0.05 mM.

(a) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions and UV–vis absorption spectrum of 20 μM KMnO4 aqueous solutions. (b) Fluorescence decay of PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions at emission wavelength of 500 nm as a function of time in the absence (black) and presence (red) of 100 μM KMnO4. The amount of PNI-MSPC microgel suspension was 2 m L with concentration C MG of 0.426 mg/m L and the corresponding concentration of viologen moieties was 0.05 mM (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
The synthesis route and chemical structure of PNI-MSPC microgels with redox actuatable size and fluorescence properties.
The limit of detection (D L), linear concentration range and response time of various strategies for detecting MnO4 − anions in aqueous solution reported in the literature.

row.entry: Fluorescence determination with N,S,P Co-doped carbon nanodot

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Concentrations of MnO4 − anions in daily drinking water and tap water from our laboratory on Yuquan Campus of Zhejiang University as well as the lake water from the west lake of Hangzhou city as determined by fluorescence measurement by using PNI-MSPC microgels as the sensor.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive and selective detection of MnO4 − in aqueous solution with fluorescent microgels

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou 310027, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory of Macromolecular Synthesis and Functionalization, Department of Polymer Science & Engineering, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou 310027, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Key Laboratory of Macromolecular Synthesis and Functionalization

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The novel functional microgels, namely PNI-MSPC microgels, were fabricated via surfactant free emulsion polymerization (SFEP) by using N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAm) as the monomer and a home-designed dichlorinated 1,1′-di[4-(vinyl)benzyl]-4,4′-pyridinium salt (MSPC2+.2Cl−) as the cross-linker. The PNI-MSPC microgels exhibited not only thermo-sensitivity but also redox actuatable hydrodynamic diameter and fluorescence properties. The PNI-MSPC microgels were successfully applied as the fluorescent sensor for ultrasensitive and selective detection of trace MnO4- anions in aqueous solutions. The presence of MnO4- anions led to the significant fluorescence quenching of the PNI-MSPC microgels with a fast response time of 5 min. The limit of detection (D L) for MnO4- anions can reach 25 nM with a linear concentration range of 0–50 μM. The co-existence of 16 other species of anions or 17 species of metal ions did not interfere with the detection of trace MnO4- anions in aqueous solutions. The potential applications of the PNI-MSPC microgels for sensing trace MnO4- anions in real world samples were also validated.

ce:section.ce:para: The detection of trace hazardous chemicals in the environments is paramount importance for the environmental protection and pollution control in an early stage. Manganese (VII), i.e. MnO4 −, is one of the hazardous chemicals and has been received great attentions because of its carcinogenic properties and extensive uses in both laboratory and industrial fields [1–4]. For example, the KMnO4-based Hummers method has been widely used for the synthesis of graphene oxide (GO), which is an important precusor for producing graphene [5–7]. The usage of KMnO4 might inevitably generate polluting species of Mn2+, MnO4 − and Mn2O7 in the environments. The excess of MnO4 − in living bodies may result in neurological disorders and disability. It is thus necessary and important to detect, determine and even real-time monitoring the trace MnO4 − in the environments. Therefore, it is essential to develop highly sensitive and selective probes for the determination of Mn (VII) in manufacturing industry, biology and ecological environment. World Health Organization (WHO) have listed a guideline value of 37 μM fo the maximum contaminant level of total manganese in water, including Mn°, Mn2+, and Mn7+ [8]. Several strategies have been developed for quantitative detection of MnO4 −, like colorimetric, flame atomic absorption spectrometry, extraction and spectrophotometric determination, phosphorescence determination, and fluorescence determination [9–13]. However, most of the reported strategies were usually time-consuming and high cost and were not suitable for routine and fast analysis. It is still worthy to develop new fast, facile yet robust methods for selective and sensitive detection of trace MnO4 − in aqueous solutions.

Microgels are cross-linked polymeric colloidal particles with size ranging from 10 to 1000 nm, which can swell in aqueous solution with long-term stability. With the suitable combination of monomers, comonomers and cross-linkers, the obtained microgels can selectively response to various external stimuli, like temperature, pH and ionic strength and so on [14–20]. The microgels have been demonstrated to have widely potential applications in various fields like bio-medical application, catalysis, and separation technology, etc. [21–25]. By introducing special functional groups within the microgel network, the obtained microgels can be applied as chemical sensors for the detection of heavy metal ions in aqueous solution [26–29]. Microgel sensor array can be also constructed for calorimetric detection and discrimination of heavy metal ions in aqueous solution [30]. However, there is not yet report that the microgels can be applied to detect trace hazardous anions in aqueous solution.

In the present work, we reported the novel functional microgels, coded as PNI-MSPC microgels, which were fabricated by using N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAm) as the monomer and a special designed viologen derivate as cross-linker, namely dichlorinated 1,1′-di[4-(vinyl)benzyl]-4,4′-pyridinium salt (MSPC2+.2Cl−), via surfactant free emulsion polymerization (SFEP), as shown in Scheme 1 . In such way, the viologen moieties were covalently fixed within the microgel cross-linked network via the quaternary nitrogen atoms, which rendered the fluorescence property of the resultant PNI-MSPC microgels and also avoided the release of free viologen moieties in aqueous solution. The obtained PNI-MSPC microgels exhibited not only thermo-sensitivity but also redox actuatable hydrodynamic size and fluorescence properties. The addition of a reductant, sodium dithionite (Na2S2O4), actuated the formation of temporal viologen radical cations (V+•) within the microgels, resulting the sudden shrunk and fluorescence quenching of the PNI-MSPC microgels. By autonomously “breathing” oxygen in air within a tunable time domain, V+• were slowly oxidized back to the original viologen dications (V2+), leading to the recovery of size and fluorescence for the PNI-MSPC microgels. Such size and fluorescence actuation of PNI-MSPC microgels were confirmed to be recyclable for multiple times by repeated addition of Na2S2O4. The PNI-MSPC microgel can be applied to detect trace MnO4- anions in aqueous solutions with high sensitivity and selectivity. The presence of MnO4- anions significant quenched the fluorescence intensity of PNI-MSPC microgel suspension immediately. The optimum detection conditions of PNI-MSPC microgels for MnO4- anions in aqueous solution were determined to be 5 min and 25 °C. A linear dependence was observed for MnO4- anions with concentration of 0–50 μM. The limit of detection (D L) for MnO4- can reach 25 nM. The possible sensing mechanism was discussed.

N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAm, 99%), 4,4′-bipyridine (BIPY, 99%), 4-vinylbenzyl chloride (4-CMS, 90%), ammonium persulfate (APS, 98%), potassium trifluoromethanesulfonate (KTFS, 99%), potassium tetrafluoroborate (KBF4, 99%), KF (98.5%), KBr (99%), LiNO3 (99%), Ce(NO3)3·6H2O (99%), Mn(NO3)2·4H2O (97.5%) were purchased from J&K Chemical Ltd. Sodium dithionite (Na2S2O4, 88%), acetone, CH3CN, KMnO4 (99.5%), KCl (99.8%), KI (99%), KNO2 (94%), K2SO4 (99%), K2SO3 (95%), K2CO3 (99%), KHCO3 (99.5%), KAcO (98%), KH2PO4 (99%), K3PO4 (97%), KCr2O7 (99.8%), Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (99.5%), AgNO3 (99.8%), Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (98.5%), Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (98%), Pb(NO3)2 (99%), Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (99%), Cd(NO3)2·4H2O (98%), Co(NO3)2·6H2O (99%), Ba(NO3)2 (99.5%), Ca(NO3)2·4H2O (99%), Cr(NO3)3·9H2O (99%), Al(NO3)3·9H2O (99%), NaNO3 (99%) and KNO3 (99%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. The aqueous analytical solutions including of 17 species of metal ions and 16 species of anions anions were prepared from their corresponidng nitrate salts and potassium salts, respectively. The analytical solutions of Mn(VII), i.e. MnO4 −, were prepared by diluting the 1 mM KMnO4 fresh stock solution shelter from light. All of the reagents were commercially obtained and used as received without further purification. Deionized water was used throughout the experiment as the solvent unless otherwise stated.

Scheme S1 ESI† shows the synthesis route of MSPC2+.2Cl−. Briefly, 4,4′-bipyridine (2.0 mmol) were stirred in CH3CN at three-necked round-bottomed flask, 4-vinylbenzyl chloride (20 mmol) were added via the addition funnel under nitrogen atmosphere. The mixture was then heated in an oil bath at 60 °C for 24 h. After cooling, the precipitated salt was collected by filtration and washed with acetone several times. The yellow solid was dried under vacuum to give purified product dichlorinated 1,1′-di[4-(vinyl)benzyl]-4,4′-pyridinium salt (MSPC2+.2Cl-). Yield: 0.7234 g, (78.6%). The structure of the cross-linker was confirmed by 1H and 13C NMR spectra, as shown in Fig. S1 ESI†.

NIPAm (2 mmol) was added into 45 m L of deionized water at 70 °C under vigorous stirring. Oxygen was eliminated by bubbling nitrogen through the reaction solution for 30 min, then 1 m L of APS (20 mg) aqueous solution was added into the reaction solution to initiate the polymerization. After 10 min, 4 m L of MSPC2+.2Cl− (0.06 mmol) aqueous solution was added into the reaction flask. The reaction was kept at 70 °C for 6 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the resultant microgels were purified by dialysis against deionized water with molecular weight cutoff of 8000–14000 dialysis tube for 6 days. The obtained microgels was coded as PNI-MSPC microgel. 5 m L PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions were freeze-dried and the mass of dried PNI-MSPC microgels was measured and used to calculate the concentration of PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions. The concentration (C MG) of PNI-MSPC microgels was determined to be about 4.26 mg/m L. The yield of the PNI-MSPC microgel was about 74.5%, which was calculated by the ratio of mass of obtained PNI-MSPC microgels to the mass of reactants, namely NIPAM, MSPC2+.2Cl− and APS.

The hydrodynamic size and zeta potential of the PNI-MSPC microgels were measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) at a scattering angle θ = 90° as a function of temperatures using a 90 Plus Particle Size Analyzer (Brookhaven Instruments Corp.). The sample solutions were equilibrated for 10–15 min at each measured temperature before measurement. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were carried out by a JEOL JEM-1230 electron microscope with an accelerating voltage at 80 kV. TEM samples were prepared by dip coating with Formvar-coated copper grids into the microgel suspensions. The grids were then allowed to dry in air at room temperature before observation. UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Cary 300 instrument (Varian Australia Pty Ltd.). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Vector 22 Bruker spectrometer. 1H and 13C NMR spectra were recorded on a 400 MHz Varian Mercury Plus NMR instrument with CD3OD as solvent. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu RF-5301PC Spectrophotometer with a Xe-lamp as the light sources. Both of excitation and emission band slits were 2 nm and the excitation wavelength of 380 nm unless otherwise specified. The fluorescence lifetimes were obtained from IRF decay with a time-correlated single-photon counting method with excitation wavelength of 380 nm. Confocal laser scanning microscopy (Zeiss CLSM 780) were carried out on a LSM 510 AxioObserver with a 405 nm laser as an excitation source. Cyclic voltammograms were performed with a CHI Instruments model of 660E potentiostat.

Fig. 1 a shows the representative TEM image of the PNI-MSPC microgels, which were spherical in shape with uniform size and a relatively narrow size distribution. The average diameter of PNI-MSPC microgels was calculated to be about 549 ± 49 nm (Fig. S2a ESI†). DLS measurement also showed that the PNI-MSPC microgels had an average hydrodynamic diameter (D h) of about 857 nm at 25 °C and a narrow size distribution with polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.098 (Fig. S2b ESI†). D h of PNI-MSPC microgels was larger than that calculated from the corresponding TEM image, indicating that PNI-MSPC microgels were swollen in water but collapsed on copper grids after drying [31].

The PNI-MSPC microgels exhibited reversible thermosensitive character. As shown in Fig. 1b, D h of PNI-MSPC microgels decreased with increasing the solution temperature from 25 to 70 °C because PNIPAm network chains became hydrophobic at elevated temperature, leading to the shrinkage and collapse of the microgels. With decreasing temperature from 70 to 25 °C, D h of the microgels increased again along the similar track of heating procedure. The volume transition temperature (VTT) of PNI-MSPC microgels was about 34 °C, which was slightly larger than the lower critical solution temperature (LCST) of linear polyNIPAm, i.e. 32 °C [32]. A broad temperature range of volume transition was observed for PNI-MSPC microgels, which was usually found in PNIPAm-related microgels [33,34]. Fig. 1c shows that the zeta potential of PNI-MSPC microgels was positive and about (+) 16.4 ± 0.3 mV at 25 °C, indicating that the microgels were water-soluble and stable in aqueous solutions [35]. Furthermore, the zeta potential of PNI-MSPC microgels increased from (+) 16.4 ± 0.3 mV to a plateau value of (+) 51.5 ± 1.3 mV when the solution temperature increased from 25 to 60 °C. It was understandable because the shrinking of PNI-MSPC microgels at higher temperature decreased the surface area of the microgels, hence leading to the larger surface potential of the microgels.

The FTIR spectrum of PNI-MSPC microgels confirmed the copolymerization of NIPAm and MSPC2+.2Cl−, as shown in Fig. 2 a. The characteristic absorption peaks at about 1550, 1450 cm−1 and 985, 800 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration and the bending vibration of benzene rings of MSPC2+.2Cl−, respectively. The FTIR spectrum of PNI-MSPC microgels clearly showed the characteristic absorption peaks of 1,4-disubstituted benzene rings and 1641 cm−1 of carbonyl groups of NIPAm, which confirmed the copolymerization of NIPAm and MSPC2+.2Cl−. Furthermore, the characteristic absorption peaks at 3035 and 1633 cm−1 of the alkene groups of MSPC2+.2Cl− were do not observed for the FTIR spectrum of PNI-MSPC microgels also confirmed the copolymerization.

The PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions and corresponding cross-linker MSPC2+.2Cl− exhibited the same characteristic UV–vis maximum absorption peak (λmax) at around 260 nm as shown in Fig. 2b, which was attributed to the aromatic π-π* transition of pyridinium rings of viologen moieties [36]. Therefore, the exact concentration of viologen moieties within the PNI-MSPC microgels could be explicitly determined via the absorption intensity at λmax by referring to the standard curve of MSPC2+.2Cl− aqueous solutions (Fig. S3, ESI†). The actual concentration of cross-linker MSPC2+.2Cl-, i.e. the concentration of viologen moieties, within the obtained PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions was then determined to be about 0.5 mM by this standard curve. Interestingly, the PNI-MSPC microgels exhibited strong fluorescence properties although the MSPC2+.2Cl- cross-linker do not gave fluorescence. Fig. S4 ESI† shows the photos of dried powders of pure MSPC2+.2Cl- cross-linker and the corresponding freeze-dried PNI-MSPC microgels under UV light illumination. No fluorescence was observed for MSPC2+.2Cl- powders. Nevertheless, strong green fluorescence was observed for the dried PNI-MSPC microgels. Fig. 2c also shows the fluorescence emission spectra of MSPC2+.2Cl- aqueous solutions and the corresponding PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions with the same concentration of viologen moieties. A distinct emission peak at about 500 nm was observed for the PNI-MSPC microgels. Viologen and its derivatives usually gave no fluorescence unless the conformational movements of viologen were restricted, resulting in the stabilization of S1 excited state of viologen [37–39]. Herein, the viologen moieties were covalently embedded in the microgel cross-linked network via the quaternary nitrogen atoms. The cross-linked network of the microgels might hinder the conformational mobility of pyridinium rings of the viologen links, which enhance the stability of S1 excited state of the viologen. To confirm and validate this conjecture, we investigated the fluorescence changes of PNI-MSPC microgel aqueous suspensions as a function of temperature, as shown in Fig. S5 ESI†. Obviously, when raising the temperature, the fluorescence intensity at maximum emission wavelength of PNI-MSPC microgels increased. With reducing the solution temperature, the intensity decreased along the similar track of the heating process. Usually, increasing the solution temperature may accelerate the intramolecular motions and lead to the decrease of fluorescence intensities. The results of Fig. S5c ESI† indicated that the collapse of PNI-MSPC microgel network at elevated temperature might further exert restriction to the excited-state viologen moieties, enhancing the stability of S1 excited state and hence leading to higher fluorescence intensities. Furthermore, the maximum emission wavelength of PNI-MSPC microgels also exhibited reversible shift with ca. 11 nm from ca. 502 nm at 25 °C to ca. 493 nm at 50 °C during the heating and cooling processes, as shown in Fig. S5d ESI†, which indicated the variation of microenvironment around the viologen moieties with temperature. The enhanced stability of viologen moieties within the collapsed PNI-MSPC microgels at elevated temperature suggested that less energy was lost for the excited-state viologen moieties before fluorescence emission. As a result, the energy of emitted fluorescence was higher at elevated temperature so that the corresponding fluorescence intensity increased and the corresponding fluorescence wavelength decreased, i.e. blue shift.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Redox actuation of size and fluorescence of PNI-MSPC microgels
ce:section.ce:para: Interestingly, the obtained PNI-MSPC microgel exhibited redox actuatable size and fluorescence properties upon addition of a classical reductant, namely Na2S2O4. A size jump of D h from around 850 nm–500 nm was observed immediately after addition of Na2S2O4 into the PNI-MSPC microgel suspension, which gradually recovered to its original value over a certain time period, as shown in Fig. 3 a. The lifetimes (time period for the shrunk PNI-MSPC microgel to recover to its original swollen state) of the PNI-MSPC microgels were dependent on the concentration of Na2S2O4 aqueous solutions. Fig. 3b shows that the lifetimes can be varied from about 3 to 26 min by regulating the concentration of initial added Na2S2O4 from 0.1 to 0.5 M, which gave the final concentrations of Na2S2O4 in the microgel suspensions from 1 to 2.5 mM. Upon addition of Na2S2O4, the viologen divalent cations within the PNI-MSPC microgel network were quickly reduced to viologen radical cations, which triggered a sudden decrease in electrostatic repulsion and loss of corresponding counter anions [40–42], leading to the shrinkage of microgel networks and hence the decrease of D h. The viologen radical cations could be slowly oxidized back to viologen dications by autonomously breathing oxygen in air. As a result, the temporal generated shrunk microgels were only in a non-equilibrium transient state and then reverted back to initial swollen state after a certain time domain, resulting in the recovery of D h to its original value. Hence, the higher concentration of Na2S2O4 led to the longer lifetime for the PNI-MSPC microgel recovering back to the original D h. The fully reversible redox process of the PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions was verified by cyclic voltammogram, as shown in Fig. S6 ESI†. During the size self-regulation period, the PNI-MSPC microgels exhibited a narrow size distribution (Fig. S7 ESI†). It was worthy to note that D h of PNI-MSPC microgels cannot completely recover to its original value if the concentration of initial added Na2S2O4 was as high as 0.5 M, as shown in Fig. 3a. The reaction of Na2S2O4 will produce reduction waste in the microgel suspensions. For higher concentration of Na2S2O4, more reduction waste will be generated. Up to certain concentration, the existence of reduction waste might affect the swelling of PNI-MSPC microgels at equilibrium so that D h of PNI-MSPC microgels cannot completely recover to its original value. This result suggested that a proper amount of chemical fuel was needed for the self-regulated modulation of size switching. Furthermore, the size switch of PNI-MSPC microgels with redox actuation was recyclable by repeated addition of fresh Na2S2O4. Fig. 3c and d clearly show the recycles of D h switch for PNI-MSPC microgels with addition of 10 μ L 0.2 M Na2S2O4.

Fig. 4 a indicated that the fluorescence of PNI-MSPC microgel suspension was completely quenched by adding Na2S2O4. Without addition of Na2S2O4, the fluorescence of PNI-MSPC microgels was stable over times (Fig. 4b). After addition of Na2S2O4, the fluorescence of PNI-MSPC microgels was switched off instantly. Interestingly, when viologen radical cations were slowly oxidized back to viologen dications by oxygen in air, the fluorescence intensity of PNI-MSPC microgels gradually increased to a plateau value again, which was depended on the concentration of Na2S2O4. The fluorescence intensities of the microgels almost recovered back to the original value after addition of 10 μ L Na2S2O4 solution with initial concentration of 0.1–0.3 M (Fig. 4b). Further increasing the initial concentration of Na2S2O4 to 0.4 and 0.5 M resulted in the slight decrease of fluorescence intensity to the primitive plateau value. Likewise, the microgels required longer times for fluorescence recovery with increasing the concentration of Na2S2O4. This switchable fluorescence of PNI-MSPC microgels was also recyclable by repeated addition of fresh aqueous solution of Na2S2O4. Fig. 4c shows the fluorescence on-off of PNI-MSPC microgels for five cycles by repeated addition of Na2S2O4 fresh solutions. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity decayed after two cycles (Fig. 4d). Possibly, the accumulation of reduction waste of Na2S2O4 quenched the fluorescence of PNI-MSPC microgels. To address this effect, we thus investigated the fluorescence changes of PNI-MSPC microgels upon repeated addition of completely oxidized Na2S2O4 waste (each cycle adding 10 μ L oxidized Na2S2O4 waste with concentration of 0.2 M), which was prepared from the fresh Na2S2O4 solutions exposed to air for one day. Indeed, the emission fluorescence intensities of PNI-MSPC microgels declined upon addition of completely oxidized Na2S2O4 waste (Fig. S8 ESI†). Furthermore, the dynamic fluorescence changes of the PNI-MSPC microgels over times can be directly visualized by using fluorescent confocal microscopy. Fig. 4e showed that the PNI-MSPC microgels with green fluorescence became invisible immediately after adding Na2S2O4 due to the fluorescence quenching. However, the green fluorescence of microgel gradually reappeared again over times and was fully recovery within 5 min. These results clearly indicated that the PNI-MSPC microgel also exhibited redox switchable fluorescence via the addition of Na2S2O4 and subsequent“oxygen breathing”in air. The properties shown in Figs. 3 and 4 suggested that the PNI-MSPC microgels might be potentially used as size and fluorescence actuators.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Label-free detection of trace MnO4 − anions with PNI-MSPC microgels
ce:section.ce:para: The PNI-MSPC microgels can sense MnO4 − anions in aqueous solution. It was found that the presence of MnO4 − caused significant fluorescence quenching for PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions. Fig. 5 a shows that the fluorescence intensity at 500 nm of PNI-MSPC microgel suspension with 0.05 mM viologen moieties was quenched by about 1.8 folds with the presence of 25 μM KMnO4. We tested the variations of fluorescence intensity of PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions before and after addition of 50 μM KMnO4 solutions at various temperatures. Fig. 6 a shows the time-based fluorescence intensity changes of PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions at 25, 35 and 50 °C, respectively, before and after addition of 50 μM KMnO4 as a function of time. It can be seen that the addition of MnO4- immediately led to significant fluorescence quenching of PNI-MSPC microgels and the MnO4- induced fluorescence quenching reached a plateau within 5 min for the three temperatures studied. It was understandable because it took times for MnO4- anions to evenly distribute in the microgel suspensions and diffuse into the microgel networks. The response time was thus defined as the time that the fluorescence intensity of PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions reached equilibrium after adding KMnO4 solutions. This result indicated that the fluorescence response time of PNI-MSPC microgel to MnO4- was about 5 min, which was much fast than that reported for tyloxapol oligomer in response to MnO4- anions, i.e. 30 min [43]. Fig. 6b shows the fluorescent spectra of PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions at 25, 35 and 50 °C, respectively, before (solid curves) and after (dot curves) addition of 50 μM KMnO4. Note that the fluorescent spectra of PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions with the presence of MnO4- anions were taken 5 min after addition of KMnO4. Fluorescent quenching was clearly observed regardless the measuring temperatures. However, blue shift of the fluorescent spectrum was observed for higher temperature. Fig. 6c shows the relative fluorescence intensity I0/I at maximum emission wavelength of PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions before and after addition of 50 μM KMnO4 as a function of temperature. It can be seen that I0/I monotonically decreased with increasing solution temperature up to 50 °C. I0/I ratio at 25 °C was about 1.4 times of that at 50 °C, indicating that PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions exhibited better detection sensitivity for MnO4- anions in aqueous solution at 25 °C. By taking the detection sensitivity, response time, and operational feasibility into account, the optimum detection conditions of PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions for MnO4- anions in aqueous solution were chosen to be 5 min and 25 °C. Fig. 5a also shows that the co-presence of 17 species of metal ions (50 μM), namely Cu2+, Ag+, Fe3+, Li+, Ce3+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Co3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Al3+, Na+, and K+ with counter anion of NO3 −, did not affect the fluorescence response of PNI-MSPC microgel to MnO4- anions (25 μM). The presence of above mentioned metal ions almost did not affect the fluorescence properties of PNI-MSPC microgel. Furthermore, Fig. 5b showed that the presence of other 16 species of interfering anions (50 μM), namely TFS-, BF4-, F-, Cl-, Br-, I-, NO3-, NO2-, SO4 2-, SO3 2-, CO3 2-, HCO3-, AcO-, H2PO4-, PO4 3-, and Cr2O7 2- with counter metal ion of K+ also did not affect the fluorescence response of PNI-MSPC microgel to MnO4- anions (25 μM). These results indicated that the PNI-MSPC microgel exhibited highly selective fluorescence response to MnO4- anions in aqueous solutions. Fig. 5c shows that the fluorescence intensity of PNI-MSPC microgel suspension decreased with increasing the concentration of MnO4 − from 0 to 100 μM. Fig. 5d plots the fluorescence qunenching ratio I0/I of PNI-MSPC microgel suspension versus the concentration of MnO4- anions ([MnO4-]). Note that I0 and I were the fluorescence intensities of the microgel suspension at 500 nm in the absence and presence of MnO4- anions, respectively. A linear relationship between I0/I and [MnO4-] was found in the concentration range of 0–50 μM. This linear relationship can be well described by using the modified Stern-Volmer equation, I0/I = K SV[Q] + C, where I0 and I are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of the quencher, i.e. MnO4- here, K SV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and [Q] is the concentration of the quencher, C is the constant of the equation [43–48]. The quenching constant, K SV was calculated to be 0.0416 μM-1 with a correlation coefficient R 2 of 0.992. The limit of detection (D L) for PNI-MSPC microgel suspension in the detection of MnO4- was calculated to be about 25 nM based on the 3α IUPAC criteria 3S b/K SV, where S b is the standard deviation of five successive blank measurements. The D L of 25 nM was far smaller than the concentration of Mn(VII) allowed in drinking water, i.e. 1.8 μM, according to the World Health Organization (WHO). The above results indicated that the PNI-MSPC microgels can selectively and sensitively response to trace MnO4- anion in aqueous solution. Table 1 summarized the D L, linear concentration range and response time of various strategies for detecting MnO4- anion in aqueous solution reported in the literature. It can be seen that this fluorescence sensing strategy with PNI-MSPC microgels achieved a smallest D L value, fast response time and a reasonable linear concentration range for the detection of MnO4- anions in aqueous solution.

The PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions was then applied to detect the trace MnO4 − anions in real-world samples. Note that there was not free viologen in aqueous solutions because the viologen moieties were fixed within the cross-linking network. Therefore, it might have not much worry about the contamination of viologen moieties to the environments by using PNI-MSPC microgels. The daily drinking water and tap water were collected from our laboratory on Yuquan campus of Zhejiang University. Lake water was gathered from the west lake in Hangzhou city and filtrated to remove suspended dust and solids. The fluorescence measurements indicated that the concentration of MnO4 − anions in the three kinds of water samples was less than the D L of PNI-MSPC microgel. Given concentrations of MnO4 − anions (2, 4 and 6 μM) were then spiked into the water samples, respectively, which were subsequently subjected to fluorescence measurements using 2 m L PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions with 0.05 mM viologen moieties. Table 2 shows that the measured concentrations of MnO4 − in water samples were consistent with the amounts of MnO4 − arbitrarily spiked in these samples. The spiking recoveries of MnO4 − anions were in the range of 99.07–103.5% and the relative standard deviations (RSD) of the three replicate water samples were below 3.1%. These results suggested that the PNI-MSPC microgel might have potential application for the detection of MnO4 − anions in real samples.

We further investigated the possible sensing mechanism of PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions towards MnO4 − anions. We first compared the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions to the UV–vis absorption spectrum of KMnO4, as shown in Fig. 7 a. It was observed that the UV–vis absorption spectrum of KMnO4 solutions exhibited multiple broad absorption peaks at around 310, 507, 525, 545 and 568 nm, which had significant overlapping with the emission band of PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions ranging from 450 to 600 nm and even partly overlapping with the excitation band of PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions from 270 to 400 nm. These results suggested that the presence of MnO4- anions in PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions can not only absorb the excitation light, which will weaken the fluorescence emission of the microgel, but also absorb the fluorescence emission light, which decrease the emission intensity of the microgel. As a result, the overall apparent fluorescence quenching of the PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions was observed with addition of MnO4- anions ( Fig. 5c). Fig. S9 ESI† shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions, KMnO4 solutions and PNI-MSPC + KMnO4 mixture. No new absorption band was observed for PNI-MSPC + KMnO4 mixture. We further measured the fluorescence lifetimes of PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions without and with the presence of MnO4- anions, as shown in Fig. 7b. The obtained fluorescence lifetimes were almost the same (Table S1 ESI†), indicating that there was not chemical interaction between PNI-MSPC microgel and MnO4- anions. The above results indicated that the sensing mechanism of PNI-MSPC microgel suspensions towards MnO4 − anions could be attributed to the so-call inner filter effect (IFE) observed in fluorometric analysis, which resulted from the absorption of the excitation and/or emission light by the certain absorber in solution or matrix [49–51].

The functional PNI-MSPC microgels with redox switchable size and fluorescence properties were successfully obtained. The actuation of size and fluorescence of PNI-MSPC microgels was achieved by addition of a reductant Na2S2O4 and the subsequent “oxygen breathing” in air. The obtained fluorescent PNI-MSPC microgels can detect trace MnO4 − anions in aqueous solution with high sensitivity and selectivity over 16 other species of anions and 17 species of metal ions. The limit of detection (D L) for MnO4 − anions was about 25 nM with a fast response time of 5 min and a linear concentration range of 0–50 μM. The sensing mechanism was attributed to the inner filter effect of MnO4 − anions because the presence of MnO4 − anions absorbed the excitation and emission lights of the PNI-MSPC microgels.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21875214 and 21674097), the second level of 2016 Zhejiang Province 151 Talent Project, and Open Research Fund of State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry (201601), Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.04.103.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Inner filter effect-based fluorescent sensing systems: a review

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zumei Zhu is currently a graduate student in Department of Chemistry, Zhejiang University, China. Her researches mainly focus on the design and synthesis of novel functional microgels for the detection of hazardous anions in aqueous solutions.

Jinqiao Xue is currently a PhD student in Department of Polymer Science and Engineering, Zhejiang University, China. Her researches mainly focus on the structure and properties of functional microgels.

Bin Wen is currently a graduate student in Department of Polymer Science and Engineering, Zhejiang University, China. His researches mainly focus on the design and synthesis of novel functional microgels for the detection of heavy metal ions in aqueous solutions.

Weiming Ji is currently a graduate student in Department of Polymer Science and Engineering, Zhejiang University, China. His researches mainly focus on the design and synthesis of new functional monomers and microgels for the detection of heavy metal ions in aqueous solutions.

Prof. Dr. Binyang Du is a full professor in Department of Polymer Science and Engineering, Zhejiang University, China. Dr. Du got his PhD degree at Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Science in Feb. 2002. After 4-year guest-researches in German Universities, he joined Department of Polymer Science and Engineering, Zhejiang University in Mar. 2006. His current researches mainly focus on the chemical sensors, polymer microgels, and the solution behavior of block copolymers.

Prof. Dr. Jingjing Nie is an associate professor in Department of Chemistry, Zhejiang University, China. Dr. Nie got her PhD degree at Department of Chemistry, Zhejiang University in Feb. 2002. After 4-year guest-researches in German Universities, she joined Department of Chemistry, Zhejiang University in May 2006. Her current researches mainly focus on the synthesis and application of polymer hydrogels and microgels.
